title: Why Does the Nice Guy Keep Losing — Until He Doesn't?

challenges.stem: You've been helping a colleague for months — covering their gaps, sharing your research. They keep taking without reciprocating. A friend says 'stop being nice.' What does Tit-for-Tat actually prescribe?

options.text: Stop helping them next time they ask (punish), but if they start reciprocating, immediately return to helping (forgive)
Cut them off permanently — they've shown their true character
Keep helping anyway because the relationship is long-term and they might come around eventually
Escalate by publicly calling out their behavior to create social pressure

challenges.explanation: Tit-for-Tat is retaliatory AND forgiving. It punishes defection immediately (stop helping next time) but doesn't hold grudges — if they start reciprocating, you return to cooperation. Permanent cutoff is too harsh (locks in mutual defection). Indefinite tolerance is too soft (signals that exploitation has no consequences). Public escalation introduces noise.

challenges.stem: In Axelrod's tournament, 'always defect' strategies scored well in individual rounds but lost overall. Complex strategies that mixed cooperation and defection also lost. What made Tit-for-Tat's simplicity its superpower?

options.text: Trust — its predictable pattern let opponents feel safe cooperating, triggering mutual gain across hundreds of rounds
Fear — opponents feared its retaliation and never tried to exploit it
Status — being known as the tournament winner gave it an advantage in later rounds
Incentives — the point system naturally rewarded simple strategies over complex ones

challenges.explanation: Tit-for-Tat's clarity created trust. When opponents could instantly predict 'if I cooperate, it cooperates back,' they chose cooperation — because the safe path was also the profitable one. Complex strategies failed because opponents couldn't build stable expectations, so they defaulted to self-protection. Readability enables trust.

pairs.back: Never defects first — starts every new interaction with cooperation
Punishes defection immediately — you cheat once, I mirror it next round
After one punishment, returns to cooperation — always offers a path back
Pattern is instantly recognizable — opponents quickly learn what to expect

pairs.right: Infinite repeated game with total reputation visibility

pairs.left: Online marketplace with anonymous one-time sellers
pairs.right: One-shot game where defection (fraud) is rational

pairs.left: Colleague who is sometimes generous, sometimes cold
pairs.right: Unpredictable strategy that destroys trust conditions

pairs.left: Rotating WWI soldiers to prevent truces
pairs.right: Converting a repeated game into one-shot interactions

pairs.left: Community expelling a cheating trader permanently
pairs.right: Making defection cost exceed any possible single-round gain

before.label: Naive or Cynical
before.text: Either I'm generous without boundaries (and get exploited) or I'm protective without generosity (and miss every opportunity for cooperation). Nice guys finish last.

after.label: Strategic Generosity
after.text: I cooperate first. I punish defection immediately. I forgive after one punishment. I make my pattern crystal clear. I'm not nice or tough — I'm readable. And readable wins in every repeated game.
